blocks:
Ayahs

وَيْلٌ لِّكُلِّ هُمَزَةٍ لُّمَزَةٍ (۱)
Woe to every scandal-monger and slanderer, (1)
ٱلَّذِى جَمَعَ مَالًا وَعَدَّدَهُۥ (۲)
who amasses wealth and counts it over. (2)
يَحْسَبُ أَنَّ مَالَهُۥٓ أَخْلَدَهُۥ (۳)
He supposes his wealth will make him immortal! (3)
كَلَّا ۖ لَيُنۢبَذَنَّ فِى ٱلْحُطَمَةِ (٤)
No indeed! He will surely be cast into the Crusher. (4)
وَمَآ أَدْرَىٰكَ مَا ٱلْحُطَمَةُ (٥)
And what will show you what is the Crusher? (5)
نَارُ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمُوقَدَةُ (٦)
[It is] the fire of Allah, set ablaze, (6)
ٱلَّتِى تَطَّلِعُ عَلَى ٱلْأَفْـِٔدَةِ (۷)
which will overspread the hearts. (7)
إِنَّهَا عَلَيْهِم مُّؤْصَدَةٌ (۸)
Indeed it will close in upon them (8)
فِى عَمَدٍ مُّمَدَّدَةٍۭ (۹)
in outstretched columns. (9)

Key Points

“Humazah” (همزة) derives from “hamz” (هَمز), meaning fault-finding through the eyes, eyebrows, and gestures. “Lumazah” (لَمز) means fault-finding with the tongue. The letter (ة) at the end of these two words is for emphasis (hyperbole), similar to “dhuhakah” (ضحكة), meaning someone who laughs excessively. “Hutamah” (حطمة) means complete crushing and breaking. “Mu’sadah” (موصدة) refers to a cavity created in a mountain whose door is firmly sealed. The intent of “’Amad Mumaddadah” (عمد ممدّدة) (long, extended pillars) is either the long nails used to nail down and imprison the people of punishment, or the massive flames of fire that are stretched out like tall pillars. In the language of the Quran, if you seek wealth after focusing on Allah, it is valuable: «قضیت الصّلاة فانتشروا فى الارض وابتغوا...» (167) But if you seek wealth instead of remembering Allah, it is criticized: «و اذا رأوا تجارة او لهوًا انفضوا...» (168) Fault-finding and taunting in every manner and form are forbidden (mamnu’) and unlawful (haram). Whether absent or present, by tongue or gesture, joking or serious, minor or major, related to work and industry or natural creation. Some individuals enjoy counting their wealth, showing it off, and being preoccupied with it, imagining that this fortune is permanent and will keep them in this world forever, and that sickness and death will never approach them. The Quran condemns this viewpoint and states: This perspective on the world makes a person destined for Hellfire. Otherwise, possessing worldly wealth and benefiting from it is not condemned. Since the end of this Surah describes the punishment for those who use sharp tongues and taunts as a covered and sealed Hellfire, and this type of punishment is for the disbelievers (Kuffar), it seems that the Surah relates to those who mock the Divine Prophets.

167) Jum'ah, 10. 168) Jum'ah, 11.

Messages

1- One of the afflictions and dangers of hoarding wealth is the humiliation of others. «همزة لمزة... جمع مالاً و عدّده»

2- Moral issues are part of religion, and a believing person must control his tongue and his gaze. «ویل لكلّ همزة لمزة»

3- We must be careful not to be deceived by the world and not to become arrogant due to worldly wealth. «یحسب انّ ماله اخلده»

4- Those who, instead of spending wealth (in charity), are focused on accumulating and hoarding assets, should await the crushing punishment of the Day of Judgment. «جمع مالاً و عدّده... لینبذنّ فى الحطمة»

5- Sharp tongues and taunting are among the major sins (Kabirah), because the promise of punishment has been given concerning them. «ویل لكلّ همزة لمزة... لینبذنّ فى الحطمة»

6- The fire that Allah kindles penetrates not only the body but also the soul and heart of the criminals. «نار اللّه الموقدة الّتى تطلع على الافئدة»

7- Human thought is incapable of grasping the realities of Hellfire and Paradise. «و ما ادراك ما الحطمة»

8- The tall pillars of fire block the path of escape for the inhabitants of Hell. «مؤصدة فى عمد ممددة»
